perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the internal laws of the State of New York, without giving effect to the principles of conflicts of laws thereof. In any dispute between the parties arising out of or relating to this Agreement, (i) each party irrevocably and unconditionally consents and submits to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of the state and Federal courts located in the State of New York, (ii) if any action is commenced in state court, then, subject to applicable law, no party shall object to the removal of such action to any Federal court located in the State of New York, (iii) each party irrevocably waives the right to trial by jury and (iv) each party irrevocably consents to service of process by first class certified mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the internal laws of the State of New York, without giving effect to the principles of conflicts of laws thereof. In any dispute between the parties arising out of or relating to this Agreement, (i) each party irrevocably and unconditionally consents and submits to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of the state and Federal courts located in the State of New York, (ii) if any action is commenced in state court, then, subject to applicable law, no party shall object to the removal of such action to any Federal court located in the State of New York, (iv) each party irrevocably consents to service of process by first class certified mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the waiver of the right to trial by jury. New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) requires that waivers of jury trials must be made knowingly, voluntarily, and intelligently. Deleting the explicit waiver increases the risk that a party might later claim the waiver was not valid.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 4102
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 4102 states the procedure for waiving a jury trial. Removing the explicit waiver creates a legal ambiguity as to whether a jury trial has been validly waived, especially if challenged later. The absence of an explicit waiver raises the risk that a party might argue they did not knowingly waive their right, contravening the intent of the CPLR to ensure informed consent.

Demand and waiver of trial by jury; specification of issues. (a) Demand. Any party may demand a trial by jury of any issue of fact triable of right by a jury, by serving upon all other parties and filing a note of issue containing a demand for trial by jury. Any party served with a note of issue not containing such a demand may demand a trial by jury by serving upon each party a demand for a trial by jury and filing such demand in the office where the note of issue was filed within fifteen days after service of the note of issue. A demand shall not be accepted for filing unless a note of issue is filed in the action. If no party shall demand a trial by jury as provided herein, the right to trial by jury shall be deemed waived by all parties. A party may not withdraw a demand for trial by jury without the consent of the other parties, regardless of whether another party previously filed a note of issue without a demand for trial by jury. (b) Specification of issues. In his demand a party may specify the issues which he wishes tried by jury; otherwise he shall be deemed to have demanded trial by jury of all issues so triable. If he has demanded trial by jury of only some of the issues, any other party within ten days after service of the demand may serve and file a demand for trial by jury of any other issues in the action so triable. (c) Waiver. A party who has demanded the trial of an issue of fact by a jury under this section waives his right by failing to appear at the trial, by filing a written waiver with the clerk or by oral waiver in open court. A waiver does not withdraw a demand for trial by jury without the consent of the other parties. A party shall not be deemed to have waived the right to trial by jury of the issues of fact arising upon a claim, by joining it with another claim with respect to which there is no right to trial by jur

(c) Waive
perturbation.justification: The original text contained an explicit waiver of the right to a jury trial. The changed text removes this waiver. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this increases the risk that a party might later claim the waiver was not valid, and the Law Explanation asserts that this absence raises the risk that a party might argue they did not knowingly waive their right. Both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 discuss 'Demand and waiver of trial by jury.' The omission of the waiver directly contradicts the need for a knowing waiver, presenting a compliance issue.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the return or destruction of the Evaluation Material, each party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality, non-use and other obligations hereunder for a period of three (3) years from the date of this letter agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the return or destruction of the Evaluation Material, each party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality, non-use and other obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause regarding the specified 3-year confidentiality period following termination of the agreement. This introduces a risk that trade secrets or confidential information could be disclosed without legal recourse after the agreement is terminated if no other laws provide protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the specified 3-year confidentiality, the contract fails to provide a clear period of protection for trade secrets, which may be necessary under New York Trade Secrets Law (N.Y. Gen. Oblig. Law § 35-d) to maintain the trade secret status of the Evaluation Material. The omission weakens the enforceability of confidentiality obligations, potentially allowing for the misappropriation of trade secrets without a clear contractual basis for redress after the agreement ends, which the NY law would seek to prevent.

perturbation.justification: The original text provided a three-year confidentiality period, which the changed text removes. The Law Explanation suggests this omission could undermine trade secret protection under New York law. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates the requested entry could not be found and Snippet 2 is empty, the Perturbation Explanation highlights the risk of disclosing confidential information without legal recourse after the agreement ends. The omission of the 3-year period creates a direct contradiction by removing a defined period of protection, which the Law Explanation argues may be necessary for maintaining trade secret status.

perturbation.original_text: Each party is aware, and will advise its Representatives who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this Agreement, of the restrictions imposed by Federal and state securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities by it or its Representatives have received material, nonpublic information (i.e., information that is (i) specific, (ii) non- public and (iii) likely to have a material effect on the market price of the issuer’s securities if and when made public (such that a reasonable investor would consider the information material in deciding whether to buy, hold or sell the issuer’s securities)) from the issuer of such securities and on the communication of such information to any other person or entity when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person or entity is likely to purchase or sell securities.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party is aware of the restrictions imposed by Federal and state securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the detailed explanation of what constitutes material, nonpublic information and the duty to advise representatives of insider trading restrictions. This increases the risk of insider trading violations because representatives might not fully understand their obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits the purchase or sale of a security based on material nonpublic information. By omitting the definition of 'material, nonpublic information' and the explicit duty to advise representatives, the agreement weakens the measures taken to prevent insider trading, creating a legal contradiction to the SEC's aim to prevent misuse of insider information for trading purposes. Omitting the explicit warning that communication of material, nonpublic information to others who are likely to trade on it is a violation goes directly against the intention of Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5.

CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the detailed explanation of what constitutes material, nonpublic information and the duty to advise representatives of insider trading restrictions. This omission weakens the measures taken to prevent insider trading. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, states that it is unlawful to 'employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud' or 'to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made... not misleading... in connection with the purchase or sale of any security'. By removing the definition of 'material, nonpublic information', the changed text increases the risk of parties unintentionally engaging in activities that could be considered a violation of Rule 10b-5 because representatives might not fully understand their obligations, thus operating as a device to defraud.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Evaluation Material being furnished to you, each party agrees that for a period of one year from the date set forth above, without the other party’s prior written consent, neither party nor its affiliates will solicit, directly or indirectly, to employ any person who is now employed by the other party or its affiliates and is directly involved in the discussions hereunder (a “Covered Person”).
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Evaluation Material being furnished to you, each party agrees that without the other party’s prior written consent, neither party nor its affiliates will solicit, directly or indirectly, to employ any person who is now employed by the other party or its affiliates and is directly involved in the discussions hereunder (a “Covered Person”).
perturbation.explanation: Removing the one-year restriction on soliciting employees involved in the transaction. While non-solicitation agreements are not always required by law, their absence can be problematic if trade secrets or proprietary information are heavily involved, potentially allowing a party to unfairly recruit key personnel with knowledge of sensitive data immediately after the agreement terminates, which could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (New York Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly codified, the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing exists in every contract under New York law. By removing the explicit one-year non-solicitation clause, the modified agreement creates a situation where one party could immediately target and recruit the other party's key employees involved in the transaction. This aggressive recruitment could be argued as a breach of the implied duty of good faith, especially if the recruited employees possess critical proprietary information gained during the evaluation process. The absence of the restriction creates a legal risk that the recruiting party acted in bad faith by undermining the value of the Evaluation Material exchanged and the trust established for the transaction, contradicting the principles of fair dealing.

perturbation.justification: The original contract included a one-year non-solicitation clause, which was removed in the changed text. The Law Explanation argues that this removal could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under New York law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both state that "Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." By removing the one-year restriction, the modified agreement opens the door to immediate recruitment of key employees, which could be interpreted as undermining the value of the Evaluation Material exchanged and violating the principle of good faith, thus creating a legal contradiction.
